{{日本の氏族
|家名 = Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko
|家紋 = <!-- 家紋の画像 -->
|家紋名称 =
|本姓 = {{ill|Shinanu clan|ja|科野氏}} (Atai)?
|家祖 = {{ill|Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|lt=Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=建五百建命}}
|種別 = {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別}}
|出身地 =
|根拠地 = Shinanu Province (later {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}})
|人物 = See [[#People|People]]
|支流 = (kabane in parentheses)<br />{{ill|Kanasashi-Toneri clan|ja|金刺舎人氏}} ({{ill|Atai (kabane)|ja|直 (姓)|lt=Atai|lt_ja=直}})<br />{{ill|Kanasashi clan|ja|金刺氏}} ({{ill|Kimi (kabane)|ja|キミ (カバネ)|lt=Kimi|lt_ja=君}}, later Atai, later {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}}, later {{ill|Sukune|ja|宿禰}})<br />{{ill|Ō clan|ja|多氏}} ({{ill|Asomi|ja|朝臣}})<br />{{ill|Osada clan|ja|他田氏}} (Atai)<br />{{ill|Osada-Toneri clan|ja|他田舎人氏}} ({{ill|Miyatsuko|ja|造}}, Atai)
}}
'''Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (''shinanu no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''shinanu kokuzō'', ''shinano no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''shinano kokuzō'') was a {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} who ruled the entire region that later became {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} ('''[[#Territory|Shinanu Province]]''').

== Overview ==
=== Name ===
"Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko" is the notation used in the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' and others. The {{ill|Jingū Bunko|ja|神宮文庫}} text of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' writes it as "'''<span style="-webkit-text-emphasis: filled circle;">Kami</span>nu no Kuni no Miyatsuko'''"<ref>''[[#taikei|Shinchō Zōho Kokushi Taikei Vol. 7]]''.</ref>.

=== Ancestry ===
* According to the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' (middle volume), '''{{ill|Kamuyaimimi-no-Mikoto|ja|神八井耳命}}''', son of {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}} (the first {{ill|emperor|ja|天皇}}), is said to be the ancestor of the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko and others.
* According to the "{{ill|Kokuzō Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}" of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇}} (the 10th emperor), '''{{ill|Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|lt=Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=建五百建命}}''' (''takeiotatsu no mikoto''), grandson of '''Kamuyaimimi-no-Mikoto''', was appointed the first Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko (or Kaminu no Kuni no Miyatsuko; see [[#Name|Name]]).

=== Clan ===
'''{{ill|Kanasashi-Toneri clan|ja|金刺部氏|lt=Kanasashi-Toneri clan|lt_ja=金刺舎人氏}}''' (''kanasashi-no-toneri-uji''; no {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}}) or '''{{ill|Osada-Toneri clan|ja|他田部氏|lt=Osada-Toneri clan|lt_ja=他田舎人氏}}''' (''osada-no-toneri-uji''; no {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}}). Of the same lineage as the '''{{ill|Ō clan|ja|多氏}}''' (''ō-uji''), descendants of {{ill|Kamuyaimimi-no-Mikoto|ja|神八井耳命}}, son of {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}; the clan is one of the oldest {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別}} clans. Some of them may have been active as {{ill|Wa|ja|倭人}}-descended {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} officials on the {{ill|Korean Peninsula|ja|朝鮮半島}}<ref name="#1">{{Cite journal|和書 |author=河内春人 |date=2017-03 |url=https://senshu-u.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/8264 |title=古代東アジアにおける政治的流動性と人流 |journal=専修大学社会知性開発研究センター古代東ユーラシア研究センター年報 |publisher=専修大学社会知性開発研究センター |volume=3 |pages=103-121 |doi=10.34360/00008258 |CRID=1390572174779544704}}</ref>. The Kanasashi-Toneri clan was later granted the kabane of {{ill|Sukune|ja|宿禰}}. The Kanasashi-Toneri clan and Osada-Toneri clan spread across the whole of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}, and even after the transition to the ritsuryō system, they served as {{ill|district magistrates|ja|郡}} of {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}, {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}}, and others.

=== Forged Genealogy ===

{{main|{{ill|Suwa clan#Origin|ja|諏訪氏#出自|lt=Suwa clan#Origin|lt_ja=諏訪氏#出自}}}}

The ''{{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇氏#上古の氏としての阿蘇氏|lt=Aso clan|lt_ja=阿蘇氏}}略 Genealogy (Variant Aso clan genealogy)'', presented to historian {{ill|Tanaka Takashi|ja|田中卓}} in {{ill|1956|ja|1956年}} ({{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 31), and the ''Shin-shi Keizu (Hafuri-ke-bon)'' discovered in {{ill|1884|ja|1884年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 17) also contain genealogies concerning the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko. However, these materials were produced by {{ill|Iida Taketoshi|ja|飯田武郷}} and {{ill|Nakata Kenshin|ja|中田憲信}} in the late {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} and early {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}} era, according to {{ill|Itō Rintarō|ja|伊藤麟太朗}}, {{ill|Fukushima Masaki|ja|福島正樹}}, {{ill|Terada Chinko|ja|寺田鎮子}}, {{ill|Washio Tetsuta|ja|鷲尾徹太}}, {{ill|Satō Yūichi (historian)|ja|佐藤雄一 (歴史学者)|lt=Satō Yūichi|lt_ja=佐藤雄一}}, and others<ref>Tanaka Takashi, ''Tanaka Takashi Chosakushū 2: Nihon Kokka no Seiritsu to Shoshizoku'' (Kokusho Kankōkai, 2012).</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=伊藤麟太朗|title=所謂『阿蘇氏系図』について|journal=信濃|volume=46|issue=8|publisher=[[信濃史学会]]|year=1994|pages=696-697}}</ref><ref>Murasaki Machiko, "A Tentative Theory on the Variant Aso Clan Genealogy" (''Hito-Mono-Kotoba no Jinruigaku: Kokubu Naoichi Hakushi Beiju Kinen Ronbunshū'', {{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}), pp. 202-218.</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://user1.matsumoto.ne.jp/~fukusima/yamakawa.htm |title=信濃古代の通史叙述をめぐって|author=福島正樹|date=2003-11-24|access-date=2022-04-15}}</ref><ref>Satō Yūichi, ''Kodai Shinano no Shizoku to Shinkō'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2021).</ref>.

{{ill|Aizawa Ryōtarō|ja|間枝遼太郎}} demonstrated that the ''Aso Clan Abbreviated Genealogy'' and the Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'' '''lack the power to clarify ancient historical facts'''<ref name="間枝遼太郎2023">{{Cite journal|和書|author=間枝遼太郎 |date=2023-08 |title=大祝本『神氏系図』・『阿蘇家略系譜』再考 : 再構成される諏訪の伝承 |journal=国語国文研究 |ISSN=02890488 |publisher=北海道大学国語国文学会 |issue=161 |pages=1-16 |CRID=1520579134373407616 |id={{国立国会図書館書誌ID|033071475}}}}</ref>.

The Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'':
* In the prose genealogy section describing the lineage from {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}} to '''Arikazu''' (during the {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} era), there are numerous places where it quotes and tampers with the text of the ''{{ill|Suwa-Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏訪大明神画詞}}'', established in {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}) by {{ill|Suwa Enchū|ja|諏訪円忠}}, and of the Maeda-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'' compiled around {{ill|Entoku|ja|延徳}} 2 ({{ill|1490|ja|1490年}}) to {{ill|Eishō|ja|永正}} 1 ({{ill|1504|ja|1504年}}) by Enchū's 5th-generation descendant {{ill|Suwa Sadamichi|ja|諏訪貞通}}.
* As a result of quoting and tampering with the ''{{ill|Suwa-Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏訪大明神画詞}}'', distorted sentences have been created that cannot exist in the original usage (the term "founder" is used for "'''Shinshi'''{{efn|In the original text of the ''Suwa-Daimyōjin Ekotoba'', "Arikazu" of the Emperor Yōmei era.}}'''is the founder of Arikazu'''{{efn|The original "Arikazu" of the Emperor Heizei era transmitted in the Suwa region of Shinano Province; in the original text of the ''Suwa-Daimyōjin Ekotoba'' "Shin-shi"}}" rather than clan names){{efn|Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'': "When the divine child was 8 years old, the venerable deity appeared and, taking off the divine garment, clothed the child, saying 'I have no body; I will use you as my body.' With a divine decree, he hid his body. This is precisely the founder of the Shin-shi Arikazu of the Miso-gi-no-Hōri. In the 2nd year of the reign of Emperor Yōmei, the divine child built a shrine at the foot of Konan Mountain."}}.
*Regarding the origin of the Shin clan's Suwa-Daimyōjin, it states: "Younger brother of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, sixth-generation descendant of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ|lt=Take-haya-Susano-o-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=健速須佐之男命}}, second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|オオクニヌシ|lt=Ōnamochi-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=大名持命}} — {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神|lt=Minakata-Tomi-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=御名方富命}} is this one." However, the view that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} was the "6th-generation descendant" of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊}} is the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' theory (in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' and ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', they are in a parent-child relationship), and the ''Kojiki'' was hardly available for people to read until {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s ''{{ill|Kojiki-den|ja|古事記伝}}''.
* There is a description that a figure named "{{ill|Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|茨木国造}} Kokoro-no-Mikoto (''kokoro no mikoto'')" was appointed Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko and later expelled. However, this is Takekoro-no-Mikoto (''takekoro no mikoto'') — whom the ''{{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'' calls Takekori-no-Mikoto, the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' calls Takekori-no-Mikoto, and the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' calls Takekoro-no-Mikoto, ancestor of the Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko — and such a peculiar notation "Kokoro" only appears in the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経}}'', a {{ill|forgery|ja|偽書}} created in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}} (reading the character 心 as "kokoro" and assigning the reading "ro" to the character 意, which is akin to 心){{efn|''Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō'' Kokuzō Hongi: "Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko. During the reign of the Takaanaho-no-Miya Seimu court, Miyatomi-Washio-no-Mikoto, son of '''Takekori-no-Mikoto''', ancestor of the Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, was appointed and granted kuni no miyatsuko."}}.
*The description that Kokoro-no-Mikoto (Takekori-no-Mikoto, i.e. Takekori-no-Mikoto in the ''Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō'') is connected with the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko also only appears in the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経}}''{{efn|''Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō'' Kokuzō Hongi: "Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko. During the reign of the Takaanaho-no-Miya Seimu court, Miyatomi-Washio-no-Mikoto, son of '''Takekori-no-Mikoto''', ancestor of the Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko, was appointed and granted kuni no miyatsuko."}}
— from these points, it has become clear that the genealogy was created after the Edo period<ref name="間枝遼太郎2023"/>.

Furthermore, the ''Aso Clan Abbreviated Genealogy'' was established by combining the Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'', the "Kanasashi Clan Abbreviated Genealogy" quoted in ''Supplemented Suwa Clan Genealogy, Sequel'' by Nobukawa Kazuhiko with supplements by Iida Kōtarō, and the genealogy of the {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇氏}} (Aizawa calls this "re-reconstruction"). In the process of combining these genealogies, a contradiction arose from two "Arikazu" figures: '''Arikazu''', the founder of the Ōhōri in {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}} 2 ({{ill|587|ja|587年}}) whose origins were in {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}} with {{ill|Taishi worship|ja|太子信仰}} incorporated, as transmitted in the Kyoto Suwa clan ({{ill|Suwa Enchū|ja|諏訪円忠}} and his 5th-generation descendant {{ill|Suwa Sadamichi|ja|諏訪貞通}}); and '''Arikazu''', the founder of the Ōhōri in {{ill|Daidō (Japan)|ja|大同 (日本)|lt=Daidō|lt_ja=大同}} 1 ({{ill|806|ja|806年}}), as transmitted in the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}} of the Upper Shrine of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and in the shinchōkan {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}. To resolve this, the "Arikazu" of Yōmei 2 (587), as transmitted to the Kyoto Suwa clan and recorded in the Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'', had his name rewritten as "Shinshi (also Kumako or Kumako)," and the legends of both "Arikazu" came to be described side by side<ref name="間枝遼太郎2023"/>.

In addition, the kuni no miyatsuko-related personal names in the first half of the prose genealogy of the Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'' (such as Takemikatomi-no-Mikoto and Morohi-Wake-no-Mikoto) are also not genealogies or names actually transmitted from ancient times; they were created in the early modern period or later, referring to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' and the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経}}'' in order to establish the identity of the shake of the Lower Shrine of Suwa-taisha in the early modern period as descendants of the Kanasashi clan and the genealogy itself<ref name="間枝遼太郎2023"/>.

== Base ==
There are various theories regarding the base of the kuni no miyatsuko. {{ill|Kobayashi Toshio (historian)|ja|小林敏男 (歴史学者)|lt=Kobayashi Toshio|lt_ja=小林敏男}} takes the view that the place name "Shinanu" is derived from "Shina" (a level difference), and locates the origin of the Shinano place name in the Hanishina-Sarashina area; since a figure who appears to be a Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko — "{{ill|Shinanu-no-Abita|ja|斯那奴阿比多}}" — appears in the Emperor Keitai section of the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', he considered the base to be the Zenkōji Plain and Ueda Basin, centered on the Hanishina-Sarashina area and including Minouchi and Chiisagata Districts<ref name="#2"> Inoue Kesao, Ushiyama Yoshiyuki (eds.), ''Ronshū: Tōgoku Shinano no Kodai Chūsei-shi'' (Iwata Shoin, 2008).</ref>. There is also a theory that the base was {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} '''{{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}'''<ref name="chizu">''[[#chizu|Nihon Rekishi Chizu: Primitive and Ancient Part II]]''.</ref>, limited to the present {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}<ref name="chizu"/>. According to the ''{{ill|Wamyō Ruijushō|ja|和名類聚抄}}'', Chiisagata District had a {{ill|gō|ja|郷#律令制の郷|lt=gō|lt_ja=郷}} called {{ill|Aso-gō|ja|安宗郷}} (''aso-''), and even today there is the {{ill|Aso Shrine|ja|安曽神社}} in Koaso (''koaso''), {{ill|Ueda, Nagano|ja|上田市|lt=Ueda|lt_ja=上田市}}. These have the same sound as {{ill|Aso|ja|阿蘇}} (''aso'') in Kyūshū, the original residence of the first Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto. However, "蘇" is "so" (class A), while "曽" is "so" (class B), so in {{ill|Jōdai Tokushu Kanazukai|ja|上代特殊仮名遣}} they are different sounds. Also, no trace of the Aso clan's migration to Chiisagata District appears in any other region's place names or history, and besides Chiisagata District, there are Aso-gō in {{ill|Bitchū Province|ja|備中国}}, {{ill|Dewa Province|ja|出羽国}}, and {{ill|Harima Province|ja|播磨国}}; since {{ill|Okinaga-Hiko-no-Ōkimi|ja|息長日子王}}, younger brother of {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}}, was the ancestor of the Asō-no-Kimi of Harima Province, these are considered unrelated<ref name="#2"/>. Within the old Aso-gō there is '''{{ill|Ikushima-Tarushima Shrine|ja|生島足島神社}}''' (''ikushima-tarushima jinja''; {{Coord|36|21|36.90|N|138|13|5.50|E|region:JP-20_type:landmark|name=Ikushima-Tarushima Shrine}}; {{ill|Shimono-gō, Ueda|ja|下之郷 (上田市)|lt=Shimono-gō|lt_ja=下之郷}}, Ueda City), presumed to have been enshrined by the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko<ref name="taikei">''[[#taikei2|Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 20: Nagano Prefecture Place Names]]''.</ref>, and the area around it is identified as the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko's seat of government<ref name="taikei"/>. There is also a view placing the kuni no miyatsuko base in the Kōhan area based on the location of the Hanishina Kofun Cluster<ref>{{ill|Fujimori Eiichi|ja|藤森栄一}}, ''Shinano Kōkogaku Sanpo'', {{ill|Gakuseisha|ja|学生社}}, 1968.</ref>.

Also, the "Shina" in the later Shinano Province '''{{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}''' and '''{{ill|Sarashina District|ja|更級郡}}''' is the same as the "Shina" in Shinanu.

=== Territory ===
The territory of the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko was the region then called '''Shinanu Province''', i.e. the entire area of the later {{ill|ritsuryō province|ja|令制国}} of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}}.

For the etymology of the place name, see {{ill|Shinano Province#Name and Origin of "Shinano"|ja|信濃国#「信濃」の名称と由来|lt=Shinano Province#Name and Origin of "Shinano"|lt_ja=信濃国#「信濃」の名称と由来}}.

The original reading was "Shinanu," which later became "Shinano" and was then written with the characters "科野" (Shinano). The ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' records that when {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}, son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大国主神}}, entered Suwa, he came to "the Suwa sea of '''Shinanu Province'''." In the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', there are names of {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} envoys sent to the imperial court in {{ill|Emperor Kinmei|ja|欽明天皇}} 14 ({{ill|553|ja|553年}}): Jōbu-Tokusotsu {{ill|Shinanu-no-Shisu|ja|科野次酒|lt='''Shinanu'''-no-Shisu|lt_ja='''科野'''次酒}}<ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Vol. 19, Emperor Kinmei 14 (553) 1st month kinoto-i day 12.</ref> and Jōbu-Naso {{ill|Shinanu-no-Shiraki|ja|科野新羅|lt='''Shinanu'''-no-Shiraki|lt_ja='''科野'''新羅}}<ref>''Nihon Shoki'' Vol. 19, Emperor Kinmei 14 (553) 8th month hinoto-tori day 7.</ref>; the first appearance of "Shinanu" in an {{ill|official history|ja|正史}} is the mid-{{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}.

In the age of the gods, {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}}, eldest son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami|ja|大国主神}} who had opposed the {{ill|Transfer of the Izumo Kingdom|ja|出雲の国譲り}}, fought {{ill|Takemikazuchi-no-Kami|ja|建御雷神}} of the Yamato side in {{ill|sumo|ja|相撲}} and was defeated; being spared, he was made to live in Suwa District of Shinanu Province. There, {{ill|Suwa Shrine|ja|諏訪神社}} was built and it came to command the rank of {{ill|ichi-no-miya|ja|一の宮}} of Shinano Province, attracting veneration as a deity of martial arts and reclamation.

{{ill|Keyhole-shaped kofun (rectangle)|ja|前方後方墳}} of the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} have been discovered at various places in Nagano Prefecture, such as {{ill|Kōbōyama Kofun|ja|弘法山古墳}} ({{ill|Matsumoto, Nagano|ja|松本市|lt=Matsumoto|lt_ja=松本市}}), {{ill|Himezuka Kofun|ja|姫塚古墳}} ({{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市}}), and {{ill|Takinomine Kofun Cluster|ja|瀧の峯古墳群}} ({{ill|Saku, Nagano|ja|佐久市|lt=Saku|lt_ja=佐久市}}), and their periods are generally considered to be roughly the same — early Kofun period (early to mid-{{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}}). Afterwards, {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} were built throughout the prefecture; in particular, in the area from southern Nagano City to northern Chikuma City, the prefecture's largest keyhole-shaped kofun — such as {{ill|Mori-Shōgunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}}, {{ill|Senryū-Shōgunzuka Kofun|ja|川柳将軍塚古墳}}, and {{ill|Kurashina-Shōgunzuka Kofun|ja|倉科将軍塚古墳}} — are concentrated. In the late Kofun period, many {{ill|Goguryeo|ja|高句麗}}-style stacked-stone tombs are distributed around Takai District.

From the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} to the Kofun period, Shinanu was the {{ill|Chikuma River|ja|千曲川}} basin centered on Sarashina-Hanishina; the central area (Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko) is said to have been from present-day {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}} (former {{ill|Kōshoku|ja|更埴市}}) — where the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun Cluster|ja|埴科古墳群}} remains, with Mori-Shōgunzuka Kofun, the prefecture's largest keyhole-shaped kofun and regarded as the tomb of the Great King of Shinanu, at its head — to southern Nagano City (former Sarashina District), where Senryū-Shōgunzuka Kofun is located<ref>{{ill|Fukushima Masaki|ja|福島正樹}}, "The Formation of Shinano Province," in {{ill|Furukawa Sadao|ja|古川貞雄}}, Fukushima Masaki, {{ill|Ibara Kesao|ja|井原今朝男}}, {{ill|Aoki Toshiyuki|ja|青木歳幸}}, {{ill|Odaira Chifumi|ja|小平千文}}, ''Nagano-ken no Rekishi'' (Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2003), p. 43.</ref>. In the genealogy, it is seen that in the 6th century {{ill|Kanasashi Maze|ja|金刺麻背|lt=Maze|lt_ja=麻背}} was reappointed Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, and this appointment is considered to indicate the movement of the kuni no miyatsuko base, which coincides with the center of the keyhole-shaped kofun shifting from the {{ill|Nagano Basin|ja|長野盆地}} (Zenkōji Plain) to the Iida Basin<ref>"Section 3: The Kanasashi Clan of Suwa, Chapter 7: The Ancient State of Suwa Shrine," ''Suwa Shishi Jōkan'', 1995, p. 704.</ref>.

Shinanu Province became the {{ill|ritsuryō province|ja|令制国}} of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} in the {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}}. As a ritsuryō kuni no miyatsuko, as of Engi 14 (914), 6 ''chō'' of {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko fields|ja|国造田}}<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%9B%BD%E9%80%A0%E7%94%B0-49958 Kokuzōden] Seisenban Nihon Kokugo Daijiten</ref> were granted (Daijōkan-fu in ''{{ill|Bessen Fusenshō|ja|別聚符宣抄}}'').

== Tutelary Shrine ==
Possibly '''{{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}''' (''suwa-taisha''; {{Coord|35|59|53.37|N|138|7|10.09|E|region:JP-20_type:landmark|name=Suwa-taisha}}), the {{ill|ichi-no-miya|ja|一宮}} of {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} located in the former {{ill|Suwa District|ja|諏訪郡}}. The {{ill|Ōhōri|ja|大祝}} of the {{ill|Lower Shrine|ja|諏訪大社#下社|lt=Lower Shrine|lt_ja=下社}} was served by the Kanasashi clan, descendants of the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, but after conflict with the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}} they were destroyed, and the kindred {{ill|Takei clan|ja|武居氏}} served until the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. However, since the Ō clan is a {{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別}} clan, the original tutelary shrine of the kuni no miyatsuko is also said to be '''{{ill|Ikushima-Tarushima Shrine|ja|生島足島神社}}''', which enshrines {{ill|imperial ancestral deities|ja|皇祖神}}.

=== Related Shrines ===
* {{ill|Shinano-Ōmiya Shrine|ja|科野大宮社}} (''shinano ōmiya sha''; {{Coord|36|23|40.04|N|138|15|28.80|E|region:JP-20_type:landmark|name=Shinano-Ōmiya Shrine}})
*: A shrine located in {{ill|Ueda, Nagano|ja|上田市}} (former {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}), {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} {{ill|sōja|ja|総社}} (with a variant theory). Traditionally said to have been founded by the first Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto.
* {{ill|Ikushima-Tarushima Shrine|ja|生島足島神社}} (''ikushima-tarushima jinja'')
*: Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami are deities with a close connection to the {{ill|imperial family|ja|皇室}}, and may indicate that the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko family — descendants of Kamuyaimimi-no-Mikoto — settled in northern Shinano<ref>"Imperial Family Clans" (2018), {{ill|Hōga Toshio|ja|宝賀寿男}}.</ref>. It may also have been the original shrine of the kuni no miyatsuko.
* {{ill|Hafuri Shrine|ja|祝神社}} (''hafuri jinja'')
*: A shrine located in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市}}, Nagano Prefecture. It enshrines {{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama-no-Mikoto|ja|天活玉命|lt=Ikumusubi-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=生魂命}}, considered the same deity as Ikushima-no-Kami.
* {{ill|Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto Shrine|ja|玉依比賣命神社}} (''tamayorihime no mikoto jinja'')
*: A shrine located in Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture. It enshrines {{ill|Tamayori-hime (Hyūga mythology)|ja|タマヨリビメ (日向神話)|lt=Tamayorihime-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=玉依比賣命}}, mother of {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}}.
* {{ill|Susuki-Mizu Shrine (Chikuma)|ja|須須岐水神社 (千曲市)|lt=Susuki-Mizu Shrine|lt_ja=須須岐水神社}}
*: A shrine located in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Its auxiliary Hafuri Shrine enshrines {{ill|Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto|ja|建五百建命|lt=Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=武五百建命}}.
* {{ill|Yamaga Shrine|ja|山家神社}} (''yamaga jinja'')
*: A shrine located in Ueda City, Nagano Prefecture. It enshrines {{ill|Kamuyaimimi-no-Mikoto|ja|神八井耳命}}, the ancestor of the kuni no miyatsuko.
* {{ill|Takeminakatatomi-no-Mikoto-Hiko-Kamiwake Shrine (Nagano, Shinshū-Shin-machi)|ja|健御名方冨命彦神別神社 (長野市信州新町)|lt=Takeminakatatomi-no-Mikoto-Hiko-Kamiwake Shrine|lt_ja=健御名方富命彦神別神社}} (''takeminakatatomi no mikoto hiko kamiwake jinja'')
*: A shrine located in Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture. It enshrines Kamuyaimimi-no-Mikoto, the ancestor of the kuni no miyatsuko.
* {{ill|Aizuhime Shrine|ja|会津比売神社}} (''aizuhime jinja'')
*: A shrine located at the foot of {{ill|Mount Saijo|ja|妻女山}} in Iwano, Matsushiro-chō, Nagano City, Nagano Prefecture. The enshrined deity {{ill|Aizuhime-no-Kami|ja|会津比売神}} is said to be the daughter of {{ill|Izuhayao-no-Mikoto|ja|出早雄命|lt=Izuhayao-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=伊豆早雄命}} (or possibly transformed from daughter of {{ill|Aichi-hayao-no-Mikoto|ja|会知早雄命}}), and is said to be the wife of the first Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto.
* {{ill|Aichi-Hayao Shrine|ja|会地早雄神社}} (''ōji-hayao jinja'')
*: A shrine located in {{ill|Sakaki, Nagano|ja|坂城町}} in {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}. Currently enshrining {{ill|Kumano-no-Kami|ja|熊野神}} and {{ill|Ōanamochi-no-Mikoto|ja|大穴持命}}, but based on its name it may originally have enshrined Aichi-Hayao-no-Mikoto.
* {{ill|Sumisaka Shrine (Suzaka)|ja|墨坂神社 (須坂市)|lt=Sumisaka Shrine|lt_ja=墨坂神社}} (''sumisaka jinja'')
*: A shrine located in {{ill|Suzaka, Nagano|ja|須坂市}}, Nagano Prefecture. Along with Ikushima-Tarushima Shrine, it appears only in the Kinai and Shinano Province. Two candidate shrines exist.

=== Tombs ===
* '''{{ill|Senryū-Shōgunzuka Kofun|ja|川柳将軍塚古墳}}'''
*: A {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Nagano City|ja|長野市}} (former {{ill|Sarashina District|ja|更級郡}}), Nagano Prefecture; considered one of the oldest kofun in the Nagano Basin (commonly: Zenkōji Plain), built in the early {{ill|4th century|ja|4世紀}}. At the time of discovery, many {{ill|bronze mirrors|ja|銅鏡}}, {{ill|beads|ja|勾玉|lt=beads|lt_ja=玉類}}, and {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were discovered.
* '''{{ill|Mori-Shōgunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}}''' ({{Coord|36|31|52.86|N|138|08|14.02|E|region:JP-20_type:landmark|name=Mori-Shōgunzuka Kofun}})
*: A keyhole-shaped kofun in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}} (former {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}}), Nagano Prefecture, belonging to the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun Cluster|ja|埴科古墳群}} (''hanishina kofun-gun''). Built in the mid-4th century, making it one of the oldest keyhole-shaped kofun in Nagano Prefecture; there is a theory that it is the tomb of the first Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.nihonjiten.com/data/263245.html |title=科野国造 （ 信濃 ）|publisher= 日本辞典|accessdate=2018-07-06}}</ref>.
*'''{{ill|Ōjizuka Kofun|ja|王子塚古墳}}'''
*: A {{ill|scallop-shaped kofun|ja|帆立貝式古墳}} in {{ill|Ueda, Nagano|ja|上田市}} (former {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}}), Nagano Prefecture; the only scallop-shaped kofun in Ueda City. Built between the mid-{{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀}} and the first half of the {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀}}.
* '''{{ill|Futagozuka Kofun (Ueda)|ja|二子塚古墳 (上田市)|lt=Futagozuka Kofun|lt_ja=二子塚古墳}}'''
*: A keyhole-shaped kofun in Ueda City, Nagano Prefecture; the only keyhole-shaped kofun in Ueda City and the easternmost in eastern Shinano. Said to be the tomb of the first kuni no miyatsuko, Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto, with a Futago Shrine on the mound enshrining Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto; however, its construction is considered to be the first half of the 6th century, later than the Mori-Shōgunzuka Kofun, which contradicts the record of his being appointed kuni no miyatsuko during the reign of Emperor Sujin.

== People ==
* '''{{ill|Takeiotake-no-Mikoto|ja|武五百建命|lt=Takeiotake-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=健五百武命}}''' (''takeiotake no mikoto'' / ''takeiotatsu no mikoto''; Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto, Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto)
*: According to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', the first Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko of the {{ill|Emperor Sujin|ja|崇神天皇|lt=Emperor Sujin|lt_ja=崇神朝}} court, said to be the 4th-generation descendant of {{ill|Kamuyaimimi-no-Mikoto|ja|神八井耳命}}.

=== Wa-lineage Baekje Officials ===
{{ill|Wa-lineage Baekje officials|ja|百済|lt=Wa-lineage Baekje officials|lt_ja=倭系百済官僚}} bearing the '''Shinanu''' {{ill|uji|ja|氏}}. They are said to be descendants of the sons and brothers of the kuni no miyatsuko who were dispatched to Korea as the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko army, born to local wives<ref>"Section 3: The Yamato Court and the Shinanu no Kuni," ''{{ill|Nagano-kenshi|ja|長野県史}} Tsūshi-hen Vol. 1: Primitive and Ancient'', 1989, p. 312.</ref>. However, unlike other Wa-lineage Baekje officials such as "Mononobe-no-Makamu-no-Muraji" and "Ki-no-Omi-Naso-Mimasa," they do not appear to have a {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}}; so there is also a theory that "Shinanu clan" here does not mean "a family of the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko," but rather that Shinanu people who migrated to Korea before the establishment of the {{ill|uji-kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} were called "X of the Shinanu people" (i.e., Shinanu is not a clan name)<ref name="#1"/>. Shinano people engaged in diplomacy because Shinano people played a certain role within the Yamato Court, which is considered to have come about because the culture of the horse — essential to military action — was transmitted to Shinano by {{ill|toraijin|ja|渡来人}}<ref name="#3">Satō Yūichi, "Kodai Shinano no Shizoku to Shinkō" (2021, Yoshikawa Kōbunkan).</ref>.
* {{ill|Shinanu-no-Abita|ja|斯那奴阿比多}} (''shinanu (no) ahita'')
*: A Baekje envoy appearing in the Emperor Keitai and Kinmei chronicles. {{ill|Kobayashi Toshio|ja|小林敏男 (歴史学者)|lt=Kobayashi Toshio|lt_ja=小林敏男}} takes the view that the place name "Shinanu" derives from "Shina" (a level difference), and locates the origin of the Shinano place name in the Hanishina-Sarashina area, considering Shinanu-no-Abita a figure from the Kōshoku area<ref name="#2"/>.
* {{ill|Shinanu-no-Shisu|ja|斯那奴次酒}} (''shinanu (no) shisu''; Shinanu-no-Shisu)
*: A Baekje Jōbu-Tokusotsu, Shitoku, Naishin-Tokusotsu appearing in the Emperor Kinmei chronicle.
* {{ill|Shinanu-no-Shiraki|ja|科野新羅}} (''shinanu (no) shiraki'')
*: A Baekje Jōbu-Naso appearing in the Emperor Kinmei chronicle.

== Descendants ==
=== Shinanu clan{{efn|However, unlike other Wa-lineage Baekje officials such as "Mononobe-no-Makamu-no-Muraji" and "Ki-no-Omi-Naso-Mimasa," they do not appear to have a {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=姓}}; so there is also a theory that "Shinanu clan" here does not mean "a family of the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko," but rather that Shinanu people who migrated to Korea before the establishment of the {{ill|uji-kabane system|ja|氏姓制度}} were called "X of the Shinanu people" (i.e., Shinanu is not a clan name)<ref name="#1"/>.}} ===
* {{ill|Shinanu-no-Tomomaro|ja|科野友麻呂}}
*: A {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}} {{ill|gōzoku|ja|豪族}}. A Baekje-lineage naturalized person; granted the surname Kiyota-no-Miyatsuko in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 5.
* {{ill|Shinanu-no-Iwayumi|ja|科野石弓}}
*: A Nara period gōzoku. A Baekje-lineage naturalized person; granted the surname Ishibashi-no-Muraji at Jū-shichi-i-jō in {{ill|Tenpyō-jingo|ja|天平神護}} 2.
* {{ill|Shinanu-no-Mushimaro|ja|科野虫麻呂}}
*: In {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji|ja|天平宝字}} 2 ({{ill|758|ja|758年}}) Month 9, he received distribution cloth from the Tōdai-ji copying bureau (he was a {{ill|hakuchō|ja|白丁}} at this time); in the 3rd year, he became a former tōgū-toneri at Shō-sho-i-ge; in the 6th year Month 2 he was summoned to the Ishiyama-in Daihannya-kyō-sho; the following month he became an ōtoneri at Shō-sho-i-ge. He later also became a sa-ōtoneri; in {{ill|Tenpyō-jingo|ja|天平神護}} 1 ({{ill|765|ja|765年}}) he is said to have been 45 years old<ref name="#3"/>.
* {{ill|Shinano-no-Hamatari|ja|信濃浜足}}
*: A Nara period gōzoku. In {{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15 ({{ill|743|ja|743年}}) Months 5 to 9, he engaged in sutra copying as a Shashoso scribe<ref name="#3"/>.

=== {{ill|Kanasashi clan|ja|金刺氏}} ===
* {{ill|Kanasashi-Toneri Wakashima|ja|金刺舎人若島|lt=Kanasashi Wakashima|lt_ja=金刺若嶋}}
*: A Nara period woman. {{ill|Nyoju|ja|女嬬}}. She was granted {{ill|Ge|ja|外}} {{ill|Jū-go-i-ge|ja|従五位下}} and the {{ill|Muraji|ja|連}} kabane.
* {{ill|Kanasashi-Toneri Hachimaro|ja|金刺舎人八麻呂|lt=Kanasashi Hachimaro|lt_ja=金刺八麻呂}}
*: A Nara period {{ill|gōzoku|ja|豪族}}. {{ill|Ina District|ja|伊那郡}} taryō. He was active in {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|ja|藤原仲麻呂の乱}}. Ge Jū-go-i-ge.
* {{ill|Kanasashi-Toneri Sadanaga|ja|金刺舎人貞長|lt=Kanasashi Sadanaga|lt_ja=金刺貞長}}
*: A {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} gōzoku. Mikawa-no-suke. Granted the {{ill|Ō clan|ja|多氏|lt=Ōnoasomi|lt_ja=大朝臣}} kabane. Ge Jū-go-i-ge.
* {{ill|Kanasashi Masanaga|ja|金刺正長}}
*: A Heian period gōzoku. {{ill|Hanishina District|ja|埴科郡}} taryō. Ge Jū-go-i-ge.

=== {{ill|Osada clan|ja|他田氏}} ===
* {{ill|Osada-no-Kunimaro|ja|他田国麻呂}}
*: A Nara period gōzoku. {{ill|Chikuma District|ja|筑摩郡}} taryō. Son of Osada Ōshima.
* {{ill|Osada Fujio|ja|他田藤雄}}
*: A Nara period gōzoku. {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}} gon-shōryō. Ge Jū-go-i-ge.
* {{ill|Osada Ōshima|ja|他田大島}}
*: A Nara period gōzoku. A {{ill|kokuzō-tei|ja|国造丁}} who commanded the {{ill|sakimori|ja|防人|lt=sakimori|lt_ja=防人}}. Composed a sakimori poem included in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|ja|万葉集}}''.
* {{ill|Osada Chiyome|ja|他田千世売}}
*: A Nara period woman. Granted 2 ranks of nobility.
* {{ill|Osada Maki|ja|他田真樹}}
*: A Heian period gōzoku. {{ill|Chiisagata District|ja|小県郡}} district administrator. Died in battle when {{ill|Taira no Sadamori|ja|平貞盛}} and {{ill|Taira no Masakado|ja|平将門}} fought near {{ill|Shinano Kokubun-ji|ja|信濃国分寺}}.

== Forged Genealogy / "Variant Aso Genealogy" ==
In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 31 ({{ill|1956|ja|1956年}}), {{ill|Tanaka Takashi|ja|田中卓}} visited the Aso family in Miyaji in search of the genealogy of the {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇氏}}. What he was provided with from the Aso family was a document called the "Variant Aso Genealogy" marked "Presented by Nakata Kenshin." Tanaka considered this to have been established in the mid-Edo period or later, but in reality it was produced during the Meiji era by {{ill|Nakata Kenshin|ja|中田憲信}}. The content of the genealogy was: with Takeiotatsu-no-Mikoto ({{ill|Takeiwatatsu-no-Mikoto|ja|健磐龍命}}) as the ancestor, on one hand it connects from the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko to the Ōhōri family of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社}}, and on the other hand, with {{ill|Aso no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|阿蘇国造}} {{ill|Hayamikatama-no-Mikoto|ja|速瓶玉命}} as the ancestor, it connects to the Aso Ōgūji family. Between them, all the answers are contained regarding the shift of administrative divisions from "{{ill|hyō|ja|評}}" to "{{ill|gun|ja|郡}}" — from "Hyōdoku" to "Gun-gi-taryō" — the granting of the surname "Uji," appointment as "Aso Gūji," and other things such as the position of the Aso clan within the ancient ritsuryō system, the beginning of the Aso Gūji, and why the Ōgūji family took the name "Uji" in the medieval period.

Originally, the Aso clan genealogy was contained in ''{{ill|Zoku Gunsho Ruijū|ja|続群書類従}}''. This is a genealogy that was established by the time of Tomotaka, son of Tomosada, recording a "Aso Keizu" that preserved the traditions transmitted down to the time of Tomosada's son, from Koreto — the legendary ancestor of the Kamakura period — through {{ill|Aso Koremitsu|ja|阿蘇惟光}} and {{ill|Aso Korezen|ja|阿蘇惟善}}; combined with a divine genealogy from {{ill|Emperor Jinmu|ja|神武天皇}} to Koreto, and copied by {{ill|Maruyama Kasumi|ja|丸山可澄}} in the {{ill|Jōkyō|ja|貞享}} era.

However, the variant genealogy does not appear prior to the establishment of the Aso clan genealogy in ''Zoku Gunsho Ruijū''. If there had been such a detailed variant genealogy, it would surely have been adopted when the Aso clan genealogy was being compiled, and no description in medieval Aso documents even hints at a variant genealogy. It is noteworthy that annotations such as "Nakata Kenshin Presented" and "Nakata Kenshin Edited" are seen in this variant genealogy, which indicates that this variant genealogy was provided from outside.

In Shōwa 58 ({{ill|1983|ja|1983年}}), {{ill|Iida Mizuho|ja|飯田瑞穂}} stated, "At an early point, I noticed several examples showing the title transition kuni no miyatsuko → gun-doku → taryō," and implicitly pointed out that the variant genealogy was a forgery: "Professional genealogy compilers have existed in every era, and it would not be far off the mark to think that such a background existed. Among them, there are also those who were kokugaku scholars like {{ill|Suzuki Matoshi|ja|鈴木真年}}, who gained reputation in that world. The transition kuni no miyatsuko → gun-doku → taryō may have been a reflection of those people's knowledge and understanding."

Also, in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 8 ({{ill|1996|ja|1996年}}), {{ill|Murasaki Machiko|ja|村崎真智子}} denied the credibility of the variant Aso genealogy. Murasaki, through classification of the variant Aso genealogy and lineage analysis, clarified that the origin of the variant Aso genealogy began with Nakata Kenshin's manuscript in the early Meiji era, and also introduced discussion of the relation between the variant Aso genealogy and the Suwa "Shin-shi Keizu," which partially resemble each other. The so-called "Shin-shi Keizu" is said to have been discovered in the former Ōhōri family of the Upper Shrine of Suwa-taisha in {{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 17 ({{ill|1884|ja|1884年}}); from the supplement to ''Shūho Suwa-shi Keizu'', {{ill|Takei Kōshige|ja|武居幸重}}'s "Aso Clan Genealogy Case," and {{ill|Miyaji Naokazu|ja|宮地直一}}'s ''Suwa-shi'', Murasaki concluded that one must conclude that the Suwa-taisha Gūji {{ill|Iida Taketoshi|ja|飯田武郷}} drafted the text and Nakata Kenshin made it into the genealogy. In other words, the Aso no Kuni no Miyatsuko family and the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko family genealogies in the variant Aso genealogy contain later forgeries, and Iida and Nakata were involved in these.

Iida, Nakata, and Suzuki Matoshi, another forger of genealogies like Nakata, were, judging from ''{{ill|Wagaku Sōran|ja|和学総覧}}'', fellow disciples of the {{ill|kokugaku|ja|国学者|lt=kokugaku scholar|lt_ja=国学者}} {{ill|Hirata Kanetane|ja|平田銕胤}}. For these kokugaku scholars, the history and relationship of gun-hyō were widely known knowledge, as in ''{{ill|Shinpen Hitachi Kokushi|ja|新編常陸国誌}}'' and elsewhere. Iida, Nakata, and Suzuki were wary of people who might suspect the forged genealogy. So they thought that annotating the forged genealogy with "examples showing the title transition kuni no miyatsuko → gun-doku → taryō" would increase the credibility of the genealogy. In fact, Tanaka, who discovered the variant Aso genealogy, carefully examined the genealogy and, while knowing that late Edo-period kokugaku scholars had knowledge of "{{ill|hyō|ja|評}}," made the optimistic judgment that "while it is not impossible to completely fabricate, there is no need to doubt to that extent."

{{ill|Aizawa Ryōtarō|ja|間枝遼太郎}} held that the theory recorded in the ''Aso Clan Abbreviated Genealogy'' that the {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇氏}} and the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko clan were of the same lineage, and the temporal consistency of titles such as "Hyōdoku," had already been refuted by the historical investigations of predecessors; on this basis, he demonstrated that the ''Aso Clan Abbreviated Genealogy'' and the Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'' '''lack the power to clarify ancient historical facts'''<ref name="間枝遼太郎2023"/>.

The Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'':
* In the prose genealogy section describing the lineage from {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}} to '''Arikazu''' (during the {{ill|Enryaku|ja|延暦}} era), there are numerous places where it quotes and tampers with the text of the ''{{ill|Suwa-Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏訪大明神画詞}}'', established in {{ill|Enbun|ja|延文}} 1 ({{ill|1356|ja|1356年}}) by {{ill|Suwa Enchū|ja|諏訪円忠}}, and of the Maeda-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'' compiled around {{ill|Entoku|ja|延徳}} 2 ({{ill|1490|ja|1490年}}) to {{ill|Eishō|ja|永正}} 1 ({{ill|1504|ja|1504年}}) by Enchū's 5th-generation descendant {{ill|Suwa Sadamichi|ja|諏訪貞通}}.
* As a result of quoting and tampering with the ''{{ill|Suwa-Daimyōjin Ekotoba|ja|諏訪大明神画詞}}'', distorted sentences have been created that cannot exist in the original usage (the term "founder" is used for an individual rather than for a clan).
*Regarding the origin of the Shin clan's Suwa-Daimyōjin, it states: "Younger brother of {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, sixth-generation descendant of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|スサノオ|lt=Take-haya-Susano-o-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=健速須佐之男命}}, second son of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|オオクニヌシ|lt=Ōnamochi-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=大名持命}} — {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神|lt=Minakata-Tomi-no-Mikoto|lt_ja=御名方富命}} is this one." However, the view that {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} was the "6th-generation descendant" of {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素戔嗚尊}} is the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' theory (in ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'' and ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', they are in a parent-child relationship), and the ''Kojiki'' was hardly available for people to read until {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本居宣長}}'s ''{{ill|Kojiki-den|ja|古事記伝}}''.
* There is a description that a figure named "{{ill|Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|茨木国造}} Kokoro-no-Mikoto (''kokoro no mikoto'')" was appointed Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko and later expelled. However, this is Takekoro-no-Mikoto (''takekoro no mikoto'') — whom the ''{{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'' calls Takekori-no-Mikoto, the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}'' calls Takekori-no-Mikoto, and the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' calls Takekori-no-Mikoto, ancestor of the Ibaraki no Kuni no Miyatsuko — and such a peculiar notation "Kokoro" only appears in the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経}}'', a {{ill|forgery|ja|偽書}} created in the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
*The description that Kokoro-no-Mikoto (Takekori-no-Mikoto) is connected with the Shinanu no Kuni no Miyatsuko also only appears in the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経}}''.
— from these points, it has become clear that the genealogy was created after the Edo period<ref name="間枝遼太郎2023"/>.

Furthermore, the ''Aso Clan Abbreviated Genealogy'' was established by combining the Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'', the "Kanasashi Clan Abbreviated Genealogy" quoted in ''Supplemented Suwa Clan Genealogy, Sequel'' by Nobukawa Kazuhiko with supplements by Iida Kōtarō, and the genealogy of the {{ill|Aso clan|ja|阿蘇氏}} (Aizawa calls this "re-reconstruction"). In the process of combining these genealogies, a contradiction arose from two "Arikazu" figures: '''Arikazu''', the founder of the Ōhōri in {{ill|Emperor Yōmei|ja|用明天皇}} 2 ({{ill|587|ja|587年}}) whose origins were in {{ill|Takeminakata-no-Kami|ja|建御名方神}} with {{ill|Taishi worship|ja|太子信仰}} incorporated, as transmitted in the Kyoto Suwa clan ({{ill|Suwa Enchū|ja|諏訪円忠}} and his 5th-generation descendant {{ill|Suwa Sadamichi|ja|諏訪貞通}}); and '''Arikazu''', the founder of the Ōhōri in {{ill|Daidō (Japan)|ja|大同 (日本)|lt=Daidō|lt_ja=大同}} 1 ({{ill|806|ja|806年}}), as transmitted in the {{ill|Suwa clan|ja|諏訪氏}} of the Upper Shrine of {{ill|Suwa-taisha|ja|諏訪大社}} in {{ill|Shinano Province|ja|信濃国}} and in the shinchōkan {{ill|Moriya clan|ja|守矢氏}}. To resolve this, the "Arikazu" of Yōmei 2 (587), as transmitted to the Kyoto Suwa clan and recorded in the Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'', had his name rewritten as "Shinshi (also Kumako or Kumako)," and the legends of both "Arikazu" came to be described side by side<ref name="間枝遼太郎2023"/>.

In addition, the kuni no miyatsuko-related personal names in the first half of the prose genealogy of the Hafuri-hon ''Shin-shi Keizu'' (such as Takemikatomi-no-Mikoto and Morohi-Wake-no-Mikoto) are also not genealogies or names actually transmitted from ancient times; they were created in the early modern period or later, referring to the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'' and the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi Taiseikyō|ja|先代旧事本紀大成経}}'' in order to establish the identity of the shake of the Lower Shrine of Suwa-taisha in the early modern period as descendants of the Kanasashi clan and the genealogy itself<ref name="間枝遼太郎2023"/>.

== Footnotes ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== Citations ===
{{Reflist|30em}}

=== Notes ===
{{notelist}}

== References ==
* ''Kokushi Daijiten'' ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}})
* {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō (historian)|ja|坂本太郎 (歴史学者)|lt=Sakamoto Tarō|lt_ja=坂本太郎}}, {{ill|Hirano Kunio|ja|平野邦雄}}, ''Nihon Kodai Shizoku Jinmei Jiten'' ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}})
* ''Nihon-shi Kō-jiten'' ({{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}})
* ''Shintō Daijiten'' ({{ill|Rinsen Shoten|ja|臨川書店}})
* Furukawa Sadao (ed.), ''Kenshi 20: Nagano-ken no Rekishi'' (Yamakawa Shuppansha, 2003)
* {{Cite book|和書|author=|editor=[[近藤敏喬]]|title=古代豪族系図集覧|year=1993|publisher=[[東京堂出版]]|isbn=4-490-20225-3|pages=92,343-352頁|chapter=|ref=keizu}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author=太田亮|authorlink=太田亮|editor=|title=姓氏家系大辞典. 第1巻|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1130845/130|accessdate=2018-07-08|year=1936|publisher=[[姓氏家系大辞典刊行会]]|page=114|chapter=|ref=seishi}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author=|authorlink=|editor=[[黒板勝美]]|title=[[国史大系#第3次：新訂増補国史大系|新訂増補國史大系]] 第7巻|edition=新装版|year=1998|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=4642003088|pages=先代舊事本紀凡例1頁,先代舊事本紀本文147頁|chapter=|ref=taikei}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author=|editor=[[竹内理三]]等|title=日本歴史地図 原始・古代編 下|year=1982|publisher=[[柏書房]]|page=290|chapter=|ref=chizu}}
* {{Cite book|和書|author=平凡社地方資料センター|editor=|title=[[日本歴史地名大系]]第二十巻 [[長野県]]の地名|year=1979|publisher=[[平凡社]]|isbn=4582490204|pages=32,281頁|chapter=|ref=taikei2}}
== Related Literature ==
* Satō Yūichi, ''Kodai Shinano no Shizoku to Shinkō'' [https://www.yoshikawa-k.co.jp/book/b583784.html] Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2021, ISBN 9784642046626

== See also ==
* {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko#List of Shokokuzō|ja|国造#諸国造一覧|lt=Kuni no miyatsuko#List of Shokokuzō|lt_ja=国造#諸国造一覧}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:しなぬのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:信濃国]]
[[Category:長野県の歴史]]
[[Category:多氏|#しなぬのくにのみやつこ]]
[[Category:金刺氏|#しなぬのくにのみやつこ]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:科野国造]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
